questions.question:
Consider functions Function_1 and Function_2 expressed in pseudocode as follows:

Function_1:
  while n > 1 do
    for i = 1 to 100 * n do
      x = x + 1;
    end for
    n = n / 2;
  end while

Function_2:
  for i = 1 to n do
    for j = 1 to n do
      x = x + 1;
    end for
  end for

Let f1(n) and f2(n) denote the number of times the statement 'x = x + 1' is executed in Function_1 and Function_2, respectively. Which of the following statements are TRUE?

A. f1(n) is in Theta(f2(n))
B. f1(n) is in O(f2(n))
C. f1(n) is in Theta(n^2 * log n)
D. f2(n) is in O(n)
questions.to_find: The asymptotic relationship between f1(n) and f2(n), the execution counts of 'x = x + 1' in Function_1 and Function_2.

understand.plain: Function_1 has a while loop that halves n each iteration (so it runs log2(n) times), and inside each iteration it runs a for loop 100*n times — but n changes each iteration. Function_2 is a simple nested loop running n*n times. We need to count total executions of 'x = x + 1' in each function and compare them asymptotically.

keywords.explain: f(n) = Theta(g(n)) means f grows at exactly the same rate as g — both an upper and lower bound.
f(n) = O(g(n)) means f grows no faster than g — g is an upper bound.
Sum of a geometric progression a + ar + ar^2 + ... When r < 1, the sum is bounded by a constant times the first term.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='520' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Function_1: f1(n)</text><text x='20' y='55' class='sub mut'>Iterations of while: log2(n)</text><text x='20' y='75' class='sub mut'>Inner for at iteration k: 100*(n/2^k)</text><text x='20' y='95' class='sub mut'>Total = 100n + 50n + 25n + ... = 200n</text><rect x='10' y='105' width='230' height='35' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='125' y='127' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>f1(n) = Theta(n)</text><text x='280' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Function_2: f2(n)</text><text x='280' y='55' class='sub mut'>Outer loop: n times</text><text x='280' y='75' class='sub mut'>Inner loop: n times each</text><text x='280' y='95' class='sub mut'>Total = n * n = n^2</text><rect x='270' y='105' width='230' height='35' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='385' y='127' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>f2(n) = Theta(n^2)</text><text x='130' y='185' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>f1(n) = O(f2(n))  since n = O(n^2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two columns showing Function_1 (while loop halving n, giving geometric series summing to Theta(n)) and Function_2 (nested for loops giving n^2). Conclusion: f1 = O(f2).

given.aim: Determine the asymptotic relationship between f1(n) (execution count in Function_1) and f2(n) (execution count in Function_2).

terms.meaning: Number of times 'x = x + 1' executes in Function_1. The while loop halves n each time, running log2(n) times. At iteration k (0-indexed), n = n_0/2^k, so inner loop runs 100*(n_0/2^k) times.
Number of times 'x = x + 1' executes in Function_2. Outer loop runs n times, inner loop runs n times for each iteration of outer loop.
terms.connects: f2(n) = n * n = n^2, so f2(n) = Theta(n^2).

terms.meaning: The total inner loop count in Function_1 is sum_{k=0}^{log2(n)-1} (n/2^k) = n * (1 + 1/2 + ... + 2/n) <= 2n (geometric series with ratio 1/2).
terms.connects: This shows f1(n) = Theta(n), much smaller than f2(n) = Theta(n^2).

given.plan: Compute f1(n) exactly using geometric series: the while loop runs log2(n) times and at step k the inner loop executes 100*(n/2^k) times, summing to Theta(n). Compute f2(n) = n^2. Then compare: n = O(n^2) so f1(n) = O(f2(n)), but n != Theta(n^2) so f1(n) is NOT Theta(f2(n)).

steps.title: Count executions in Function_1
steps.apply: The while loop: n starts at n, and at each iteration n is halved, continuing while n > 1. So the loop body runs for n, n/2, n/4, ..., 2 (assuming n is a power of 2, log2(n) iterations). At iteration k, the inner for loop runs 100*(n/2^k) times. Total: f1(n) = 100*n * sum_{k=0}^{log2(n)-1} (1/2)^k = 100*n * (1 - (1/2)^(log2 n))/(1 - 1/2) = 100*n * 2*(1 - 1/n) = 200n*(1 - 1/n). So f1(n) = Theta(n).
steps.note: The geometric series with ratio 1/2 converges: the total inner loop work is dominated by the first (largest) iteration, giving O(n) overall.

steps.title: Count executions in Function_2
steps.apply: The outer loop runs exactly n times (i = 1 to n). For each outer iteration, the inner loop runs exactly n times (j = 1 to n), incrementing x once. Total: f2(n) = n^2. So f2(n) = Theta(n^2).
steps.note: This is the classic O(n^2) nested loop pattern.

steps.title: Compare f1(n) and f2(n) asymptotically
steps.apply: Since f1(n) = Theta(n) and f2(n) = Theta(n^2): (A) f1(n) = Theta(f2(n))? No, because Theta(n) != Theta(n^2). (B) f1(n) = O(f2(n))? Yes, because n = O(n^2). (C) f1(n) = Theta(n^2 * log n)? No, f1(n) = Theta(n). (D) f2(n) = O(n)? No, f2(n) = Theta(n^2). Only B is TRUE.
steps.note: f1 is O(f2) because n grows slower than n^2. But f1 is not Theta(f2) because f2 grows strictly faster.

questions.formula_note: Geometric series sum for loop analysis; comparison of polynomial growth rates (n vs n^2) using Big-O and Theta definitions.

questions.question:
Let T(n) be the recurrence relation defined as follows:
  T(0) = 1,
  T(1) = 2, and
  T(n) = 6*T(n-1) - 8*T(n-2)  for n >= 2

Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

A. T(n) = Theta(2^n)
B. T(n) = Theta(n*2^n)
C. T(n) = Theta(3^n)
D. T(n) = Theta(n*3^n)
questions.to_find: The asymptotic closed-form for the recurrence T(n) = 6T(n-1) - 8T(n-2), T(0)=1, T(1)=2.

understand.plain: We have a linear recurrence relation with constant coefficients. To solve it, we find the characteristic equation of the recurrence. The roots of this characteristic equation determine the closed-form solution. The dominant root determines the asymptotic growth rate.

keywords.explain: For a recurrence T(n) = a*T(n-1) + b*T(n-2), the characteristic equation is r^2 = a*r + b, whose roots give the general solution T(n) = A*r1^n + B*r2^n.
keywords.example: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) has characteristic equation r^2 = r + 1, giving r = (1 +/- sqrt(5))/2.

keywords.explain: When the characteristic equation has roots r1 and r2 with |r1| > |r2|, then T(n) grows like r1^n asymptotically.
keywords.example: If roots are 3 and 2, then T(n) = A*3^n + B*2^n grows like Theta(3^n).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='480' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='28' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>T(n) = 6T(n-1) - 8T(n-2)</text><text x='240' y='58' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Characteristic equation: r^2 - 6r + 8 = 0</text><text x='240' y='82' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Roots: r = (6 +/- sqrt(36-32))/2 = (6 +/- 2)/2</text><rect x='80' y='95' width='140' height='35' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='150' y='117' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>r1 = 4</text><rect x='260' y='95' width='140' height='35' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='330' y='117' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>r2 = 2</text><text x='240' y='158' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>T(n) = A*4^n + B*2^n = Theta(4^n)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Characteristic equation r^2 - 6r + 8 = 0 has roots r1=4 and r2=2. General solution is A*4^n + B*2^n, dominated by 4^n = (2^2)^n = 2^(2n). Wait — need to verify against options. Options say Theta(2^n) or Theta(3^n). Let me re-examine.

given.aim: Solve the recurrence T(n) = 6T(n-1) - 8T(n-2) with initial conditions T(0)=1, T(1)=2 and determine its asymptotic growth.

terms.meaning: Initial conditions needed to pin down the exact constants A and B in the general solution.
terms.example: These let us solve: A + B = 1, 4A + 2B = 2.
terms.connects: Solving gives A and B; the dominant term determines asymptotic class.

terms.meaning: A second-order linear recurrence with constant coefficients.
terms.example: Fibonacci: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) is also second-order.
terms.connects: Characteristic equation method gives exact closed form.

terms.meaning: Roots r of r^2 - 6r + 8 = 0 give the basis for the general solution T(n) = A*r1^n + B*r2^n.
terms.connects: The larger root r1 = 4 dominates; but the initial conditions may force A = 0, making the dominant term 2^n.

given.plan: Write and solve the characteristic equation r^2 = 6r - 8. Find roots r1, r2. Write general solution T(n) = A*r1^n + B*r2^n. Substitute initial conditions T(0)=1, T(1)=2 to find A and B. Identify the asymptotic class from the dominant nonzero term.

steps.title: Set up and solve the characteristic equation
steps.apply: The recurrence T(n) - 6T(n-1) + 8T(n-2) = 0 has characteristic equation r^2 - 6r + 8 = 0. Factoring: (r - 4)(r - 2) = 0. Roots: r1 = 4, r2 = 2. General solution: T(n) = A * 4^n + B * 2^n.
steps.note: Two distinct real roots give a general solution as a linear combination of 4^n and 2^n.

steps.title: Apply initial conditions to find A and B
steps.apply: From T(0) = 1: A * 4^0 + B * 2^0 = A + B = 1. From T(1) = 2: A * 4^1 + B * 2^1 = 4A + 2B = 2. From first equation: B = 1 - A. Substituting: 4A + 2(1 - A) = 2 => 4A + 2 - 2A = 2 => 2A = 0 => A = 0. Then B = 1. So T(n) = 0 * 4^n + 1 * 2^n = 2^n.
steps.note: The initial conditions force A = 0, eliminating the 4^n term completely. The exact closed form is T(n) = 2^n.

steps.title: Determine asymptotic class
steps.apply: Since T(n) = 2^n exactly (we can verify: T(0) = 1 = 2^0 check, T(1) = 2 = 2^1 check, T(2) = 6*2 - 8*1 = 12 - 8 = 4 = 2^2 check), the asymptotic growth is Theta(2^n). Answer A is correct.
steps.note: Even though the characteristic roots are 4 and 2, the specific initial conditions collapse the solution to exactly 2^n with A=0.

questions.formula_note: Characteristic equation method for solving linear recurrences with constant coefficients; initial conditions determine which roots contribute to the closed form.

questions.question:
Consider the following two functions:

g1(n) = n^2  for 0 <= n < 10,000
         n^3  for n >= 10,000

g2(n) = n    for 0 <= n < 100
         n^2  for n >= 100

Which of the following is true?

A. g1(n) is O(g2(n))
B. g1(n) is Omega(g2(n))
C. g2(n) is O(g1(n))
D. g2(n) is Omega(g1(n))
questions.to_find: The correct asymptotic relationships between the piecewise-defined functions g1(n) and g2(n).

understand.plain: Both g1 and g2 are piecewise functions that change behavior at certain breakpoints. For large n (which is what asymptotic notation cares about), g1(n) = n^3 (since n >= 10000 eventually) and g2(n) = n^2 (since n >= 100 eventually). So for asymptotic analysis, we compare n^3 versus n^2: g1 grows faster, meaning g1 is Omega(g2) and g2 is O(g1).

keywords.explain: A function defined by different expressions on different intervals. For asymptotic analysis, only the behavior as n -> infinity matters.
keywords.example: g1(n) = n^2 for small n but n^3 for large n; asymptotically it behaves like n^3.

keywords.explain: For large enough n, the piecewise pieces with small n become irrelevant. The behavior for n >= breakpoint determines the asymptotic class.
keywords.example: g2(n) = n for n < 100 but n^2 for n >= 100, so g2(n) = Theta(n^2) asymptotically.

keywords.explain: f(n) = Omega(g(n)) means f grows at least as fast as g — g is a lower bound for f.
keywords.example: n^3 = Omega(n^2) since n^3 >= n^2 for all n >= 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='500' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='500' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='28' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Asymptotic behavior (large n)</text><rect x='20' y='45' width='210' height='70' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='125' y='68' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>g1(n)</text><text x='125' y='88' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>= n^3 for n >= 10,000</text><text x='125' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>g1(n) = Theta(n^3)</text><rect x='270' y='45' width='210' height='70' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='375' y='68' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>g2(n)</text><text x='375' y='88' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>= n^2 for n >= 100</text><text x='375' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>g2(n) = Theta(n^2)</text><text x='250' y='148' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>n^3 grows faster than n^2</text><text x='250' y='170' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>g1 = Omega(g2)  and  g2 = O(g1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes showing g1(n) = Theta(n^3) for large n and g2(n) = Theta(n^2) for large n. Since n^3 grows faster, g1 = Omega(g2) and g2 = O(g1).

given.aim: Determine which asymptotic relationships hold between g1(n) and g2(n) for large n.

terms.connects: For large n (n >= 10,000), g1(n) = n^3, so asymptotically g1(n) = Theta(n^3).

terms.meaning: g2(n) = n for 0 <= n < 100 and n^2 for n >= 100.
terms.connects: For large n (n >= 100), g2(n) = n^2, so asymptotically g2(n) = Theta(n^2).

terms.meaning: For all n >= 1: n^3 >= n^2, and in fact n^3/n^2 = n -> infinity, so n^3 grows strictly faster.
terms.connects: g1 = Omega(g2) (g1 grows at least as fast) and g2 = O(g1) (g2 is upper-bounded by g1). But g1 is NOT O(g2) and g2 is NOT Omega(g1).

given.plan: For asymptotic comparison, extract the dominant term of each piecewise function (the piece that applies for large n). Then compare n^3 (from g1) with n^2 (from g2). n^3 dominates n^2, so determine which of O/Omega/Theta relationships hold.

steps.title: Identify asymptotic behavior of g1(n) and g2(n)
steps.apply: For g1(n): For n >= 10,000, g1(n) = n^3. The small-n piece (n^2 for n < 10,000) is bounded by a constant times n^3 for large enough n (since 10,000^2 < 10,000^3). So g1(n) = Theta(n^3). For g2(n): For n >= 100, g2(n) = n^2. Similarly, the small-n piece is negligible for large n. So g2(n) = Theta(n^2).
steps.note: The asymptotic class of a piecewise function is determined by the piece active for large n, not by the small-n pieces.

steps.title: Check each option
steps.formula_raw: n^3 vs n^2: lim_{n->inf} n^3/n^2 = n -> infinity
steps.apply: A. g1(n) = O(g2(n))? Would require n^3 = O(n^2), i.e., n^3 <= c*n^2 for some constant c. But n^3/n^2 = n is unbounded. FALSE. B. g1(n) = Omega(g2(n))? Requires n^3 = Omega(n^2), i.e., n^3 >= c*n^2 for large n. Since n^3/n^2 = n >= 1 for n >= 1, this holds with c = 1. TRUE. C. g2(n) = O(g1(n))? Requires n^2 = O(n^3), i.e., n^2 <= c*n^3 for large n. Since n^2/n^3 = 1/n -> 0, this holds trivially. TRUE. D. g2(n) = Omega(g1(n))? Would require n^2 = Omega(n^3), i.e., n^2 >= c*n^3. But n^2/n^3 = 1/n -> 0, so no constant c > 0 works. FALSE.
steps.note: B and C are both TRUE. This is consistent: g1 grows strictly faster than g2, so g1 dominates (Omega from g1's perspective, O from g2's perspective).

solution.result: B and C: g1(n) is Omega(g2(n)) and g2(n) is O(g1(n))

questions.formula_note: Asymptotic analysis of piecewise functions: only the large-n piece matters; comparison of polynomial growth rates n^3 vs n^2 using Big-O and Omega definitions.
